title: Input & Data Types
description: Using input(), storing user input, converting data types, and printing input values with messages.

steps.title: Do Now Activity
steps.content:
Do Now Activity

5 minutes - from last lesson

Answer these quick questions from the variables lesson.

steps.title: The input() Function
steps.content:
The input() Function

input() asks the user to type something. Store the answer in a variable so the program can use it later.

name = input("Enter your name")
print("Hello", name)

Click run to see what happens

The prompt goes inside the brackets. The user's answer is stored in the variable on the left.

steps.title: Level 13 - Choose the Input Code
steps.content:
Level 13 - Choose the Input Code

Choose the correct Python code to ask for input and store it.

Choose the correct code to get input:

steps.title: Typing Input Code
steps.content:
Typing Input Code

The pattern is variable name, equals sign, then input().

city = input("Enter your city")
hobby = input("Enter your hobby")

Click run to see what happens

In the next level, you will type the whole input line yourself.

steps.title: Level 14 - Type the Input Code
steps.content:
Level 14 - Type the Input Code

Read each prompt and type the correct input statement.

Write input code (e.g. name = input("...")):

steps.title: Comments with Input
steps.content:
Comments with Input

A comment can explain what information the program is asking for.

# Input name
name = input("Enter name")

Click run to see what happens

For the next level, put the comment on line 1 and the input code on line 2.

steps.title: Level 15 - Comment and Input
steps.content:
Level 15 - Comment and Input

Write a useful comment, then write the matching input statement.

Write a comment (#) then the input code:

steps.title: Input and Output
steps.content:
Input and Output

After storing input in a variable, you can print it with a message.

food = input("Food?")
print("I like", food)

Click run to see what happens

Use a comma inside print() to separate the message from the variable.

steps.title: Level 16 - Input and Print
steps.content:
Level 16 - Input and Print

Choose the code that asks for input and then prints it with a message.

Choose the code that inputs AND prints:

steps.title: Data Types
steps.content:
Data Types

A data type tells Python what kind of value it is working with.

"Hello" # String
5 # Integer
5.4 # Float
True # Boolean

Click run to see what happens

A value in quotes is always a string, even if it looks like a number.

steps.title: Level 17 - Choose the Data Type
steps.content:
Level 17 - Choose the Data Type

Choose the correct data type for each value.

Choose the correct data type:

steps.title: Type Conversion
steps.content:
Type Conversion

input() always gives back a string. Convert it when you need a number.

age = int(input("Enter age"))
height = float(input("Enter height"))

Click run to see what happens

int() is for whole numbers. float() is for decimal numbers.

steps.title: Level 18 - Convert the Type
steps.content:
Level 18 - Convert the Type

Type the Python conversion code for each variable.

Write Python code to convert the variable:

steps.title: Stage 3 Ready
steps.content:
Ready for Stage 3

You have now practised every skill that appears in the Stage 3 exam.

Input strings

name = input("Enter name")

Input numbers

age = int(input("Enter age"))

Convert types

float(score)

Print input

print("Name:", name)

Click run to see what happens

steps.title: Exam 3: Input & Data Types
steps.content:
Exam 3: Input & Data Types

Work through all questions. This checks input(), data types, type conversion, and output with print().

steps.title: Endless Practice
steps.content:
Endless Practice

Random questions from across the Input lesson. Answer 5 questions correctly in a row to complete this step.

Streak: 0 Best: 0

quizQuestions.q: What does input() do in a Python program?
options: Asks the user to type something

quizQuestions.q: What data type does input() return before conversion?
Which line stores text typed by the user in city?
Which line is best for storing a whole-number age from the user?

What is printed if the user types Mia?

name = input("Name: ")
print("Hello", name)

What happens if the user types 7?

age = input("Age: ")
print(age + 1)

What does this print if the user types 7?

age = int(input("Age: "))
print(age + 1)
Which conversion is used for decimal numbers?
Ask the user for their name, store it, and print a greeting.
Write one line that stores text typed by the user in a variable called city.
Ask the user for a whole number and print that number plus one.
Write one line that asks for a decimal height and stores it in height as a number.
Ask the user for a food and print a sentence using what they typed.
